behaviors.description: A file placed inside a subdirectory of the watched root is processed, not only files placed directly in the root directory.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is real filesystem watching recursion (OS event emission + watcher backend config). A mocked watcher can fake subdirectory events and hide misconfigured/non-recursive real watch behavior. Higher levels are disqualified by behavior text: no AI/ML decision and no multi-agent sequence.
behaviors.test_description: Start the real daemon against a real temp root directory, create a nested subdirectory, write a file inside it, and assert exactly one transformed output appears for that nested file within timeout.

behaviors.description: The deduplication logic correctly discards a second processing request for the same file path within a configurable time window.
behaviors.justification: Behavior text targets dedup logic for same path + time-window rule, which is local state/time comparison and does not inherently require OS events, subprocesses, or network. Rejected higher level: no concrete text requiring real filesystem interaction for this specific assertion.
behaviors.test_description: Call the dedup component twice with the identical normalized path and controlled clock values inside the configured window; assert first request accepted and second rejected, then accepted again after window expiry.

behaviors.description: When the operating system emits multiple filesystem events for a single file write, the daemon processes the file exactly once.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on OS duplicate event patterns for one write. In-process fakes can miss backend-specific burst/coalescing behavior, so mocking can hide real failures. Higher levels disqualified: no AI/ML output dependency, no multi-agent chain.
behaviors.test_description: Run daemon on a real watched directory and perform a write pattern known to trigger multiple events (create+append/rename); verify pipeline invocation/output occurs exactly once for that file.

behaviors.description: If the watched directory is removed and then recreated while the daemon is running, the daemon resumes watching the new directory without a restart.
behaviors.justification: Requires real directory lifecycle events (delete/recreate) and re-subscription behavior of the watcher backend under live OS conditions. Mocked event streams can falsely pass and hide watcher-handle invalidation issues. Higher levels disqualified by behavior text: no model judgment and no agent pipeline.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon watching a real directory, delete that directory, recreate it with same path, then add a new file and assert it is processed without restarting daemon.

behaviors.description: When multiple files arrive at the same time, they are processed in the order determined by their creation timestamps.
behaviors.justification: Ordering correctness depends on real arrival concurrency plus filesystem timestamp semantics/precision. Pure in-process stubs can mask OS metadata behavior and event timing races. Higher levels disqualified: no AI/ML-dependent correctness and no multi-step agentic flow.
behaviors.test_description: With real daemon and filesystem, create several files near-simultaneously with controlled creation order, then assert processing/output order matches their observed creation timestamps.

behaviors.description: A file that is still being written to when the arrival event fires is not picked up for processing until writing is complete.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at real file-write/event timing boundary (partial writes, flush timing, watcher event timing). Mocking write completion or events can hide premature pickup bugs. Higher levels disqualified by behavior text: no AI model output and no agentic multi-step chain.
behaviors.test_description: Start daemon on a real directory, open a file and write it in chunks with delays while keeping stream open, trigger arrival event naturally, and assert processing starts only after final close/complete-write condition.
